data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: Blue Chips With 9%+ Yields Getting Too Risky
data.description:
Investors turn to blue-chip high-yield dividend stocks because they want to be able to enjoy attractive and ideally growing income over the long term through thick and thin. However, the recent tariff announcements by the Trump administration have called into question the safety of quite a few blue-chip dividend stocks, given the severity of them and the potential for it to lead to a severe recession. On top of that, the growing odds of recession have also greatly boosted the market's expectations of Federal Reserve rate cuts, with Mr. Market now pricing in about five rate cuts this year. This, in turn, will have consequences on businesses that have exposure to short-term interest rates. In this article, I am going to discuss two blue-chip stocks and share why I think that their dividends are increasingly at risk of being cut.

Risky Dividend #1

The first stock I am going to discuss is a blue-chip BDC (BIZD) called Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). It has one of the highest quality underlying portfolios with 98% of its investments in first-lien senior secured debt, very low non-accruals at just 0.3% on an amortized cost basis, and an investment-grade balance sheet. It is backed by the Blackstone (BX) credit team, which is one of the most impressive players in the credit space in the world, as BX itself is the largest alternative asset manager in the world.

It has also posted strong results since going public by growing its net asset value per share, its dividend per share, posting strong returns on equity, low non-accruals, and overall general total return outperformance. However, I have warned recently that its stock price had gotten ahead of itself, and that it was now overvalued. This warning proved prescient as, over the past week, it has plummeted by over 14%, significantly underperforming better-priced peers that I was more positive on by a considerable amount.

Now that its price-to-NAV multiple is back at a more reasonable level, I am still not overly bullish on the stock, as I believe its dividend is now facing a more risky outlook over the next year. In Q4, it generated $0.84 per share in net investment income while paying out a $0.77 per share base dividend. While that 109% coverage ratio looks pretty reasonable for a BDC, especially given that BDCs are expected to pay out 90% of their taxable income in order to maintain their BDC status, the macro clouds forming on the horizon indicate that BXSL's base dividend may not be sustainable. This is because, in addition to the potential for a rise in non-accruals due to growing recession concerns from the Trump tariffs, the market is now also pricing in a high likelihood of interest rates falling by 100 basis points by the end of the year.

As the below snippet from its 2024 10-K illustrates, a 100 basis point decline in interest rates would provide a roughly $100 million hit to its net investment income, given that BXSL generated $183 million in Q4 NII, which annualizes to $732 million.

That indicates a 13.7% reduction in net investment income by the end of the year simply due to interest rates declining. This does not take into account potential additional headwinds from a rise in non-accruals, as well as a potential loss in fee income from a potential decline in deal-making activity due to the tariffs causing a rise in uncertainty.

On top of that, BXSL has very low fixed interest rate debt maturing beginning in January 2026, which it will likely have to refinance at higher rates, especially if interest rates only decline by 100 basis points between now and the end of the year. This would likely provide another headwind, further reducing their net investment income.

When all that is put together, it appears that BXSL's current base dividend is unlikely to be sustainable if the Fed indeed cuts four times this year, especially if there is even a modest uptick in non-accruals due to economic weakness. As a result, while I think BXSL is a fine BDC as BDCs go, its base dividend coverage ratio is a little bit too tight for comfort, and if current macro headwinds on the horizon persist, it will likely have to contend with a base dividend reduction sometime over the next year.

Risky Dividend #2

Another blue-chip dividend stock whose dividend is likely going to come under increasing pressure moving forward is LyondellBasell Industries (LYB). LYB has a strong dividend growth track record, as it has increased its payout every year since it started paying one in 2011. It boasts a solid mid-single-digit dividend CAGR in recent years that has kept up with the high inflation post-COVID quite nicely.

On top of that, on a normalized basis, it covers its dividend quite easily, as even in 2024, as the business began to face some challenges, its payout ratio was quite conservative at just 57%, and it typically has a very high cash conversion ratio at between 83% and 98% cash conversion levels since 2019. On top of that, it has a fortress balance sheet with an investment-grade credit rating and $3.4 billion of cash and cash equivalents on hand as of the end of 2024, with a fairly modest 1.8 times net debt to adjusted EBITDA.

That being said, the chemical sector is getting hit hard right now, and while LYB has managed to outperform peers like Dow Inc. (DOW) by a considerable margin, it is still down by over 40% over the last year alone.

This is because there is weak demand and high-cost pressures on the business that are pinching its profits. As a result, it suffered meaningful downgrades recently, including UBS slashing its 2025 and 2026 EBITDA forecast by over 10% for each year. As a result, it will likely generate much less free cash flow in the coming years, especially if it sustains its current more aggressive CAPEX profile as it tries to enter the advanced plastics recycling and sustainability business. And of course, it has a huge cash pile, which should be able to support its dividend even if it does not fully cover its dividend in the near term. But at some point, the company is going to have to make a decision about what to do about its dividend if conditions do not improve, especially if they continue to deteriorate based on current headwinds from tariffs, energy price movements, and general oversupply in the marketplace.

This is particularly troublesome because the US tends to export a considerable amount of polyethylene to China, and this could be disrupted due to the ongoing trade war between the two countries. As a result, while LYB has been executing well, has a very strong balance sheet at the moment, and based on a backward look at its results, has a very well-covered dividend that management even very recently reiterated its intent to continue growing, the growing macro storm may simply be too much and force the company to abandon its dividend growth ambitions and potentially even cut its dividend.

Therefore, while I still like the company as part of a diversified portfolio due to its strong underlying fundamentals and recent execution, as well as its long-term growth potential and a very attractive yield based on a normalized earnings environment, investors should be very careful and manage their exposure accordingly. I think this is very important, as current macro headwinds could force management to slash the dividend, which could result in significant further downside for the stock.

Of course, management can also cut back on growth capex spending if it wants to in order to sustain the balance sheet and dividend, and there is also a decent chance that current headwinds could be lifted sometime in the coming months or years. On the other hand, there is no guarantee that management, despite LYB's strong dividend growth track record and management's recent comments in support of continuing to pay and grow the dividend, will continue to prioritize the dividend moving forward if headwinds persist.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, there are massive headwinds facing the global economy right now due to the uncertainty caused and potential major reduction in business and economic activity and investment due to the huge tariffs being put in place by the Trump administration. While we do think this is creating some exciting buying opportunities due to broad-based selling in the markets, as we recently discussed here, there are other blue-chip dividend stocks whose previously thought-to-be-safe payouts are now being challenged. Maintaining proper portfolio diversification and guarding against such dividend cuts is a priority of ours in our portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources: Solid Natural Gas Prices Help Its Near-Term FCF
data.description:
Over half (approximately 54%) of Mach Natural Resources' (NYSE:MNR) production is natural gas, compared to around 23% oil. Natural gas strip prices have held up better than oil strip prices recently, so Mach is still projected to generate around $2.31 per unit in 2025 free cash flow at current strip.

Mach's legacy asset production has been declining a bit, though, so I can see its 2026 free cash flow ending up a bit lower. At current 2026 strip (including $60 WTI oil), Mach's 2026 free cash flow may be around $1.75 per unit, while at $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, that may increase to around $2 per unit.

I've lowered my long-term WTI oil price expectation to $70, which results in Mach's estimated value being trimmed to $15 to $17 per unit. This is a $1.75 per unit reduction compared to its estimated value at $75 WTI oil. Mach now looks slightly undervalued for a longer-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Mach did not change its production guidance for 2025 despite its Flycatcher acquisition adding a modest amount (estimated at +1% to +2%) to its 2025 production.

I had previously modeled Mach's 2025 production including the additional volumes from Flycatcher, but have decided to model it based on its original guidance now.

The Flycatcher acquisition probably gives Mach a bit of room to trim capex later in 2025 in response to mediocre oil prices while still meeting its full-year guidance.

The current strip for 2025 involves slightly under $64 WTI oil and $4 NYMEX natural gas, and at those commodity prices I project Mach to generate $936 million in revenues after hedges.

Mach has around 42% of its 2025 oil production hedged at an average of $70.75 and approximately 42% of its 2025 natural gas production hedged at an average of $3.67.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 6,752,500 $62.75 $424
NGLs 6,752,500 $21.25 $143
Gas 95,812,500 $3.60 $345
Midstream Operating Profit $17
Hedge Value $7
Total Revenues $936

I have assumed that Mach's 2025 capex budget comes in at the low-end of its guidance range (with the potential budget trimming that I mentioned above).

This would result in Mach generating $273 million ($2.31 per unit) in 2025 free cash flow at current strip prices.

$ Million
Lease Operating $188
Gathering and Processing $93
Taxes Other Than Income $50
Cash G&A $32
Cash Interest $40
Capex $260
Total $663

Debt And 2025 Distributions

Mach reported having $510 million outstanding under its new credit facility as on March 12, 2025. However, Mach's $0.50 per unit quarterly distribution for Q4 2024 was due to be paid out on March 13, 2025. That distribution payment should have been approximately $59 million.

Mach likely has some cash on hand though (it had $106 million in cash on hand at the end of 2024), so it may have been able to make that distribution without further credit facility borrowings.

Mach's new credit facility has $750 million in commitments and matures in February 2029 now.

Pro forma for various transactions, Mach may have started 2025 with approximately $466 million in net debt.

If Mach pays out $2 per unit in 2025 distributions, it would end 2025 with $430 million in net debt (not including the effect of any working capital changes). This would be leverage of 0.75x, which is reasonable to me.

Future Free Cash Flow

Largely due to declining production, Mach's free cash flow (and thus its distribution) seems reasonably likely to be lower in 2026 than the $2.31 per unit that I am projected for 2025.

At current strip (roughly $60 WTI oil and $4.25 Henry Hub natural gas) for 2026, Mach may end up with around $1.75 per unit in free cash flow. At my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas instead, this would improve to around $2.00 per unit in free cash flow. This assumes a capex budget of around $250 million, with 2026 production having a mid-to-high single digits decline compared to 2025.

Mach may make more acquisitions to help offset the production declines from its legacy assets.

Notes On Valuation

I've reduced Mach's estimated value to $15 to $17 per unit, which is a reduction of $1.75 per unit compared to before. This is mostly due to the effect of changing my long-term WTI oil price estimate from $75 to $70. I have kept my long-term natural gas price estimate at $3.75.

I've reduced my estimate of Mach's value by around 10% compared to a few months ago, while its unit price is down closer to 20%. Thus, I am now modestly positive on Mach at its current price.

Conclusion

Mach's production is primarily natural gas, so a 2025 scenario with mid-$60s oil and roughly $4 natural gas still results in it being projected to generate around $2.31 per unit in free cash flow. This should help support a distribution of around $2 per unit for the year, allowing for a bit of debt reduction.

Mach has typically been spending less than maintenance capex, though, so its legacy production is expected to decline into 2026. This will likely result in lower free cash flow in 2026, although Mach may make further acquisitions to offset the legacy production declines.

I now estimate Mach's value at $15 to $17 per unit, based on my updated long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Medicare Bump Lifts Insurers
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Health insurers rally after CMS increases payment to Medicare Advantage plans. (00:21) Nvidia (NVDA) closes deal to acquire startup Lepton AI: report. (01:41) China pledges to 'fight to the end' against new U.S. tariffs, escalating the trade dispute. (02:38)

This is an abridged transcript.

Shares of leading health insurers rose in post-market trading Monday after the U.S. Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) announced it has substantially raised government payments to Medicare Advantage compared to an initial proposal.

In January, the CMS issued its Advance Notice for the next calendar year, proposing to raise government payments to Medicare Advantage plans by an average of 4.33%, an increase of over $21B compared to 2025.

Per the agency's final rate decision on Monday, payments to Medicare Advantage companies will rise by 5.06% on average from 2025 to 2026, translating to an increase of 2.83 percentage points since the Advance Notice or over $25B increase in MA payments.

The move follows a recent decision where Medicare coverage for expensive obesity medications was declined.

Leading health insurers are rallying in reaction to the announcement. Humana (NYSE:HUM) is up about 10%, Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV) is up 6%, UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), the largest insurer in the Medicare Advantage market is up 5.6%, and Clover Health (NASDAQ:CLOV) is up 3.7%.

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) has closed its acquisition to buy artificial intelligence startup Lepton AI, The Information reported.

The deal to acquire Lepton AI, which resells Nvidia's graphics processing units in a cloud service, was worth “hundreds of millions of dollars,” the news outlet added. Lepton AI's co-founders, Yangqing Jia and Junjie Bai, will stay on at Nvidia now that the deal has closed, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter.

Talk of an acquisition popped up late last month.

Nvidia declined to comment when contacted by Seeking Alpha.

Lepton competes with companies like Together AI in the business of renting out Nvidia GPUs. Founded two years ago, Lepton AI secured an $11M seed round in May 2023 from CRV and Fusion Fund. Meanwhile, Together AI, a key competitor in the server rental space, has raised over $500M in venture capital, despite being only about a year older than Lepton.

President Trump's renewed tariff threats on Monday have prompted China to vow a 'fight to the end,'.

China sharply criticized the U.S.'s escalating tariff threats as "blackmail".

Beijing's commerce ministry stated it would "resolutely take countermeasures to safeguard its rights and interests" if the U.S. proceeds with additional tariffs, calling Trump's actions "a mistake on top of a mistake." The ministry further declared that China would "never accept this" and would "fight to the end.”

This strong condemnation came after Trump threatened a new 50% tariff on Chinese goods effective Wednesday, on top of the current 54%, unless Beijing retracted its retaliatory tariffs – a 34% levy on all U.S. imports starting Thursday, announced in response to a prior U.S. tariff hike.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Vietnam will buy defense goods from U.S. to reduce trade gap, seeks 45-day tariff delay

Trump says EU must buy $350B of US energy to get tariff relief, rejects ‘zero-for-zero’ proposal

BlackRock’s Fink sees ‘buying opportunity’ during market selloff

Catalyst watch:

  • The two-day Stifel Virtual Targeted Oncology Forum will include participation from Compugen (CGEN), Zymeworks (ZYME), Innate Pharma (IPHA), and Revolution Medicines (RVMD).

  • Atlassian (TEAM) will hold its three-day Team25 event in Anaheim, California. CEO Mike Cannon-Brookes will be one of the keynote speakers.

  • Paris Blockchain Week will begin. Featured speakers include Yves Choueifaty of TOBAM, Anthony Scaramucci of Skybridge, and Jean-Marie Mognetti of CoinShares.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $60/barrel. Bitcoin is flat at $79,000. Gold is up 0.8% at $3,005.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 1.7% and the DAX is up 1.6%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) +10% – The stock surged after the company delivered mixed Q1 results, surpassing profit expectations.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 11:00 am Treasury Buyback Announcement

  • 1:00 pm 3-Yr Note Auction

data.title: YETI: Beloved Brand With Too Many Risks At The Moment
data.description:
To say it’s been a volatile week for the markets would be a massive understatement. Trump’s new tariffs have severely shaken global markets as the S&P 500 was down over 5% Friday and is down nearly 14% YTD. It’s been even worse from the Nasdaq, which is now in bear market territory as it’s down over 17% YTD.

Many retailers and consumer discretionary stocks, like Nike (NKE) and Lululemon (LULU) have been especially hammered. YETI Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:YETI) hasn’t been exempt from the market bloodbath, as YETI’s stock is down more than 7% this week and is down over 20% YTD.

In the fall, I stated YETI was a buy in part because of the company’s international growth. I am still a believer in the business, yet I’m not looking to rush to add shares given investors are now in a much more uncertain environment.

Let’s dig into YETI's recent financial results and discuss how Trump's tariffs could impact the business in the months (and perhaps) years to come.

Impact of Trump’s Tariffs and Future Risks

First off, let’s discuss the countries that were hit hardest by Trump’s tariffs. As Trump mentioned (or illustrated by that now infamous large tariff board) the United States is placing a 34% tariff rate on China, 49% on Cambodia, 46% on Vietnam, 36% on Thailand, 32% on Taiwan, and 24% on Japan.

As I noted in my prior article, YETI was already working to move production outside of China. Management stated on the prior earnings call that at the end of fiscal year 2024, 20% of global drinkware production would take place outside of China and the goal was to get that number up to 50% by the end of 2025.

As YETI noted in their 10K filing, in 2024, for coolers and equipment products 36% are produced in two large manufacturing sites and for drinkware products 74% are produced at those two sites. The 10K doesn’t go into specifics on the locations of those sites but given the commentary from management I’d assume one or both sites are located in China. As you can see below graphic from a recent investor presentation, all of the drinkware manufacturing sites are in eastern Asia:

On the last earnings call, there were very limited discussions surrounding tariffs, as it was obviously a fluid situation. On their Q1 2025 earnings call, it will be interesting to hear how much progress was made in diverting production to other sites such as Mexico or Italy. Clearly, YETI’s management has been making plans around these tariffs, particularly those impacting China. Yet, it’s clearly a significant material risk which the company also noted in their 10K, stating Trump’s tariffs could cause margin erosion and loss of customers if YETI is forced to pass the price increase to consumers.

Another negative ramification I could see to the business is the delay of product launches. As management noted during the last earnings call, the company is expecting more growth in the second half of the year, and one of the factors of that growth is product launches. Here’s what YETI’s CFO Mike McMullen had to say about why the business is forecasting more growth in the back half of 2025, “...We think it'll be, essentially flat – or drinkware in total will be flat in the first half, down in Q1. But as we look to the second half, I think there's a couple of things to call out. One, we do have the pacing of our innovation is much more second half weighted, this year. And I think one of the drivers of that is, all the work we're doing around our supply chain and diversification of that, I mean, that is, it does have an impact.”

As you can see this supply chain work does impact product launches and depending on the amount of progress that was made in Q1 this could determine if YETI can launch their products in the back half of 2025 on time or if some will have to pushed back into 2026 or beyond as management focuses on diverting production to limit the impact of the tariffs.

Financials

I think Trump’s tariffs will impact the cost of goods sold far more than it will impact revenue, as the majority of YETI’s revenues are generated domestically. As you can see from the table below, although international revenue has been growing, it still only accounted for roughly 19% of total revenue:

Also, YETI’s biggest international presence is currently in Canada and Australia. Two countries which weren’t as impacted by Trump’s tariffs as many others. On the earnings call, management called out expanding into Europe (specifically the U.K.) as well as growing awareness of the brand in Japan. Japan was hit with a higher tariff as I noted above, but generally these are all more “friendly” countries compared to say China or Russia, so hopefully country leaders could negotiate and reach reasonable terms.

One positive for YETI is that the company has diverse products from different revenue channels. As you can see below, nearly a decade ago basically all of YETI’s sales came from their Wholesale channel, now DTC accounts for 60%:

Also, both channels grew compared to fiscal year 2023. For the full year, DTC revenues came in at roughly $1,087 million, which is a 9% increase compared to the prior year. Wholesale revenue came in at $742 million, which is a 12% compared to 2023.

Similarly, from a category perspective, both Drinkwear and Coolers and Equipment grew compared to 2023. In 2024, Drinkware generated $1,094 million in revenue, which was a 7% increase compared to the prior year. Coolers and Equipment generated revenue of $698 million, which is a 17% increase compared to 2023.

If we zoom out and take a look at the big picture, YETI has done a great job of growing revenue and gross profit as you can see below:

Even with SG&A expenses increasing, YETI’s revenue growth has outpaced costs, resulting in continued growth in net income and earnings per share over the last three years.

YETI has also further benefited investors by buying back roughly 6% of all outstanding shares in 2024 for roughly $200 million. The company still has $450 million available remaining under their current Share Repurchase Program.

YETI still has an excellent balance sheet, as you can see below:

The company still has enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities. Furthermore, the company’s long-term debt balance has continued to decline while the company’s retained earnings balance has increased over the past year. Although YETI’s cash balance is down I’m fine with management using that cash to buy back shares and make appropriate acquisitions.

Expansion of a Beloved Brand

Thus far, I’ve discussed the numerous negatives associated with Trump’s tariffs and the potential ramifications. Yet, I am by nature an investor that likes to focus on the long-term, so I'd like to note what YETI has going in their favor and how the brand can continue to grow after the dust settles on this tariff drama.

First and foremost, YETI is a popular, beloved brand. This statement from CEO Matt Reintjes on the company’s latest earnings call illustrates the fact that YETI products are loved by consumers, “Our most recent US owner study completed in December underscores the strength of our brand. 2024 was the seventh consecutive year that 95% of YETI owner surveyed responded they have recommended YETI to peers, highlighting our incredible brand loyalty and a testament to our products. Moreover, the study shows that our consumers want us to be in the newer categories we're scaling in particular bags and travel.”

YETI also has far more social media followers than rivals Stanley and Hydro Flask, as you can see below:

As Reintjes noted above, YETI has numerous opportunities to continue to expand their product line. In 2024, YETI launched 24 new products and had a record number of color introductions. YETI has a particularly good opportunity to expand into travel and packs as the company can take advantage of their Mystery Ranch acquisition. Regarding this particular opportunity, here is what Reintjes had to say, “We have a big opportunity in front of us with a massive global addressable market across premium bags, packs and luggage and exciting launches that highlight our unique designs and capabilities, all leveraging our strong commercial engine and brand.”

Beyond packs and travel gear, YETI purchased some IP relating to a powered cooler system which could lead to future innovation for the company’s cooler portfolio and YETI will have opportunities in M&A as well. I think an opportunity such as Solo Stoves (DTC) could be a potential fit underneath YETI’s production umbrella, just as one example.

With product innovation, M&A or a combination of the two, YETI has numerous ways in the company can continue to grow in the years to come.

Valuation

YETI has a valuation grade of a "B-" at Seeking Alpha:

I still believe price to earnings is a good valuation metric for YETI, given it’s a profitable, established company. YETI's forward P/E ratio (Non-GAAP) is at 10.40 which is below the sector median of 13.32, and it's also far below YETI's five-year average. As can see below, YETI's current P/E ratio is at its lowest level in a year:

Looking at YETI's valuation another way, I have updated my reverse discounted cash flow model using the latest cash flow figures while keeping a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3%. Based on current estimates, which likely aren’t taking Trump’s new tariffs into account, most analysts believe YETI's stock will continue to grow in the mid-single digits for the next several years. Taking a more bearish stance, if YETI can only grow at a rate of 3% which, I believe is conservative, then YETI is currently undervalued:

Conclusion

I remain a believer in YETI as the company is a beloved brand and one with diverse products. YETI has a nice omnichannel approach to their business and has opportunities to add new wholesale partners and expand their product lines.

YETI is likely undervalued at the moment, but I'm not in a rush to add, and I'll be waiting to hear if any potential deals are reached relating to Trump's tariffs. I'd also like to wait until management can provide more color on how the company has been working to move production out of China.

data.title: White Brook Capital Partners Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
White Brook Capital Partners as a commingled investment fund is winding down. Almost all positions have been sold and the account will sit in cash until it is dispersed to partners. White Brook Capital will remain and continue to manage separately managed accounts. So far this month, White Brook Capital Partners is down but has outperformed the midcap index as we continued to build cash.

Less than a week ago I was optimistic we had a uniquely positioned portfolio for the next few years and the likelihood of outperformance and positive performance was exceptional. The tariff announcement this past week, along with its calculation and implementation, however, confirmed what I thought was a low probability downside risk. It is clear that the economic environment is likely to be exceptionally unpredictable this year. Unless there’s a clear reversal in policy, in the next year, I think we’ll see something similar to the early days of Covid where this administration is willing to look past facts in order to operate in a parallel reality. Having done it once before, that’s a tough environment to invest in midcaps.

I have given everything I have to make White Brook Capital Partners a success, but the performance for the past 3 years has not met expectations. While I believe our relative performance would be positive this year, I now think there’s little chance to generate significant positive returns in a midcap strategy in the near term. White Brook Capital Partners is returning capital to investors.

I believe that, like during Covid, there will be opportunities to build generational wealth during the next several years. If we shorted stocks, we’d be able to match the volatility of the market and look to aggressively buy when individual stocks got exceptionally cheap. But White Brook Capital Partners does not short stocks or hold instruments that short stocks, to match against long positions and defray the volatility. As a long only partnership, we can only sit in cash to lower volatility. I’d recommend having a larger cash allocation for the next year, and if given the opportunity, I’d do that, earn yield, and wait for individual opportunities that were beyond compelling across market caps. But I do not have a crystal ball and I wanted to give each partner a chance to decide that for themselves.

The process of unwinding the portfolio is mostly complete. We sold a large percentage of the portfolio early on Thursday and the process will end in the next couple days. The funds will sit in cash until it can be disbursed to you. There is a final audit that needs to take place, as well. The administrator or I will reach out with specifics to return capital. It will hopefully be a relatively quick process.

White Brook Capital as a firm is not shutting down and will continue to manage separately managed accounts custodied at Charles Schwab or Interactive Brokers. I believe recent international trade moves are without precedent and short on rationale and therefore have acted quickly and defensively to protect your capital. Separately managed accounts have eliminated those positions that I believe are most sensitive to the difficult macroeconomic challenges that I believe are occurring and will continue to occur in the country as long as the administration stays on its current path. Market commentators talk about uncertainty in the marketplace, but the level of business uncertainty today hinders short and long term decision making across the board, whether it’s for a company making a new investment or just maintaining current efforts, whether to try to rewire the supply chain at significant cost, or sit tight, raise prices, and hope presidential passions change. It’s difficult for management teams to operate and make decisions today and even more so for investors to accurately divine the future cash flows and strategic position that those businesses may have with imperfect information.

Should prices become so compelling, or the investing environment begin to clarify, I would look to redeploy, but how and when that is, is unclear. I look forward to having individual conversations with clients as you have time. In the meantime cash will be a significant portion of the portfolio.

With the closure of the White Brook Capital Partners and what I believe will in time be an attractive environment, White Brook Capital will have three strategies moving forward.

White Brook Capital’s Ethical Index

Outlined in our last insights post, the Ethical Index is a diversified passive investment based on the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) . It strips out vice and defense companies and those that have profited from the recent and continuing wars - leaving the index with 397 companies. It is more zealous in those aims, particularly the latter, than available options I have seen. For those who wish to strip out financial companies, that can be done as well. It has only started on April 1st and is performing in line with the S&P 500 as designed, through this downside volatility. The track record couldn’t be shorter, but there’s nothing in its construction that should result in significant divergence from the S&P 500 unless the above exclusions significantly outperform or underperform. I am happy to help clients directly or if you’d like to put me in touch with your Investment Advisor to find an arrangement that works - with them.

White Brook Capital Small Cap

The Small Cap Strategy targets small and microcap companies that we believe can grow strongly in a relatively short amount of time. Our first incubated account, started at the beginning of the year, is up over 100% this year, but that record is too short to have significant confidence in this extremely volatile strategy. These stocks are characterized by high analyzability, but low short term earnings confidence, and the opportunity for investment is measured in the hundreds of thousands and low millions of dollars in companies with market caps of less than 400 million. Each investment should be thought of as short term (1-2 years) with returns targeted at 100%, but with significant risk. We continue to only incubate this strategy.

White Brook Capital All Cap Discretionary

The All Cap Strategy targets all market caps and will hold a mix of ETFs (including the Ethical Index - at request), money market funds, and single stocks to generate returns where 60%-75% of the portfolio’s returns will be due to the allocation to “the market” or cash. The rest of the return profile will be due to single stock selection.

Unless a special situation arises - as it may with the small cap strategy - where a commingled fund is necessary - moving forward everything will be done in separately managed accounts custodied at Schwab or IB.

Again, I look forward to having individual conversations with clients as you have time. It is an honor to manage your investments, I thank you for your support, and will strive to continue to earn your trust.

Sincerely,

Basil F. Alsikafi | Portfolio Manager | White Brook Capital, LLC

All investments involve risk, including loss of principal. This document provides information not intended to meet objectives or suitability requirements of any specific individual. This information is provided for educational or discussion purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a solicitation to buy or sell securities. The information contained herein has been drawn from sources which we believe to be reliable; however, its accuracy or completeness is not guaranteed. This report is not to be construed as an offer, solicitation or recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities herein named. We may or may not continue to hold any of the securities mentioned. White Brook Capital LLC and/or their respective officers, directors, partners or employees may from time to time acquire, hold or sell securities named in this report. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions or holdings discussed were or will prove to be profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or will equal the investment performance of the securities discussed herein

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Revolution Medicines: Overpriced RAS-Addicted Cancers Maven
data.description:
This is my first Revolution Medicines (NASDAQ:RVMD) article. Revolution is an ambitious clinical stage pharma. It has managed to generate a market cap of ~$6 billion.

In this article, I will reference the following from Revolution's 02/26/2025:

  1. Q4 2024 and full year 2024 financial results and corporate progress press release;
  2. Q4 2024 earnings call;
  3. Q4 2024 10-K; and from its
  4. 02/2025 website presentation titled "On Target to Outsmart Cancer".

As clearly called out in the 10-K, Revolution is a highly speculative investment.

In common with hundreds of other clinical stage biotechs, Revolution's 10-K calls out that it has:

  1. no products approved for commercial sale;
  2. not generated any revenue from sales of products;
  3. incurred losses in each year since its inception (10/2014 in the case of Revolution)
  4. no demonstrated ability to manufacture a commercial-scale product, or arrange for a third party to do so on its behalf, or
  5. conduct sales and marketing activities necessary for successful product commercialization.

Cry me a river...that is all very much de rigueur for clinical stage biotechs. The things that make Revolution cringeworthy are its:

  1. giant escalating losses of $600.1 million, $436.4 million, and $248.7 million, for the years ended December 31, 2024, 2023 and 2022, respectively;
  2. $1.7 billion accumulated deficit as of 12/31/2024; and
  3. outsized market cap of ~$6 billion (it has fallen to $5.82 billion in the market selloff during the last few days).

Given all this, the question that jumps to the fore is "where's the beef?" I have rummaged around to try to find it. The usual suspects are an exciting late-stage pipeline, sterling liquidity, and/or some unrecognized asset. We can assess from the following how Revolution stacks in these regards.

Revolution's pipeline focuses on RAS-addicted cancers.

The 10-K describes Revolution's niche in biotech development as:

Our RAS(ON) Inhibitors are based on our proprietary tri-complex technology platform, which enables a highly differentiated approach to inhibiting the active, GTP-bound form of RAS, which we refer to as RAS(ON). We are developing a portfolio of compounds that we believe were the first RAS(ON) Inhibitors to use this mechanism of action. We believe that direct inhibitors of RAS(ON) suppress cell growth and survival and are less susceptible to adaptive resistance mechanisms recognized for RAS(OFF) inhibitors.

Presentation slide 7 below sets out Revolution's pipeline consisting of two RAS inhibitor registrational trials, 10 early-stage trials plus an unspecified number of preclinical trials:

A recent NCI press release describes the role of RAS proteins in cancer as:

RAS genes are the second most frequently mutated genes in cancer, and mutant RAS proteins are key drivers of some of the deadliest cancers, including nearly all pancreatic cancers, half of colorectal cancers, and one-third of lung cancers. Decades of research have shown that mutant RAS proteins promote the development and growth of tumors by activating specific proteins at the cell surface, creating a constant stream of signals telling cells to grow.

In terms of Revolution's registrational trials, clinicaltrials.gov characterizes them as:

  1. RASolute (NCT06625320) a phase 3 trial to evaluate the safety and efficacy of daraxonrasib (RMC-6236) compared to standard(s) of care [SOC] for patients with previously treated metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [PDAC] with estimated enrollment of 460 and study completion date of 12/2027;
  2. RASolve (NCT06881784) a phase 3 trial to evaluate the safety and efficacy of daraxonrasib compared to docetaxel in patients with previously treated locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] with estimated enrollment of 420 and primary completion date of 12/2027.

The foregoing is not the whole picture. During the Call CEO Goldsmith provided an interesting gloss to the Revolution story. He outlined Revolution's priorities going forward to:

  1. execute pivotal trials with daraxonrasib monotherapy in patients with previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer and NSCLC;
  2. advance daraxonrasib into earlier line randomized pivotal trials in patients with PDAC;
  3. generate sufficient data to inform development priorities for the mutant selective inhibitors, elironrasib (RMC-6291) and zoldonrasib (RMC-9805) and prepare to initiate one or more pivotal trials either as monotherapy or in a drug combination and a number of options are under consideration.

In reference to priority 1, he noted strong interest among patients and investigators leading to encouraging trial enrollment. Revolution is actively adding new enrollment sites; it expects to substantially complete enrollment in its trial this year to enable an expected data readout in 2026.

He had less to say about the RASolve NSCLC trial, merely confirming that activation of investigational sites was ongoing. The critical need in PDAC has led to more focus on that indication compared to NSCLC.

In regard to priority 2 above Goldsmith noted that Revolution expected:

...to initiate a trial in first-line metastatic disease comparing a reference arm of patients treated by chemotherapy to two investigational arms, one with patients treated with daraxonrasib monotherapy and one with patients treated with daraxonrasib plus chemotherapy. Based on single-agent data and preliminary chemotherapy combination data, we are optimistic that both treatment arms containing daraxonrasib have the potential to become important therapeutic options for patients living with metastatic pancreatic cancer.

He further noted plans to finalize the trial design later this year for a phase 3 trial in patients with first-line metastatic PDAC when it has sufficient safety and tolerability data.

Ample liquidity supports Revolution's significant cash burn.

The 10-K includes the following table of cash flows for years 2022, 2023 and 2024:

I usually consider a company's annual net cash provided by (used in) operating activities as a fair analog for its annual cash burn. In this case, Revolution's latest annual cash burn by that metric is $557,436. The tricky one is moving from cash burn to cash runway, insofar as their so many moving parts to figuring cash runway.

In Revolution's case it provides the above Presentation slide 27 footnote 2 showing that its cash runway extends to H2, 2027 based on its current operating plan.

Given its highly speculative prospects Revolution's ~$6 billion market cap is excessive.

Revolution has a herky-jerky price path, as shown by its price chart below. Its apex came on 11/11/2024 when it hit an intraday high of $62.40. This followed closely behind its Q3/2024 annual report with the following PDAC reports:

Phase 3 pivotal trial underway evaluating RMC-6236 in patients with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), supported by compelling clinical profile

First clinical results for RMC-9805, a RAS(ON) G12D-selective inhibitor, demonstrate encouraging safety, tolerability and antitumor activity in patients with PDAC harboring a KRAS G12D mutation

The enthusiasm quickly waned, as shown by the chart below.

Its Q4, earnings did nothing to stem its pullback from its 11/11/2024 peak. As I write on 04/06/2025 it's caught in the riptide of tariff selloffs. How it will fare once the smoke clears is a known unknown. My expectation is that it will continue its downward trend until it is able to show positive results from its 2026 PDAC data readout.

Risks to a "Sell" thesis for Revolution

My "Sell" rating is informed among other things by the inherent risks in clinical stage biotech, which are being multiplied by the current tariff induced selloffs. In this regard, there can be no assurance that the recent general market selloff will not reverse with an equal but opposite ferocity, lifting Revolution along with the rest of the market.

Similarly, Revolution could reverse its slide on its own merits. There is wide interest in the RAS driven cancer space, as illustrated by the 10-K's "Competition" section; it discloses dozens of companies invested in various aspects of this subject. Such companies make up a candidate pool that could become interested in lucrative collaborations with or acquisitions of Revolution.

Further, it is noteworthy that nine of the 13 Wall Street analysts covering Revolution have "Strong Buy" ratings on the stock; the remaining four have "Buy" ratings. Wall Street analysts' average price target for the stock is $72.33 for a 130.94% upside. In other words, my dour view of the situation is just that; there are plenty of dissenters.

Conclusion

It is maddeningly difficult to predict future price trajectories for clinical stage biotechs; this leads to "Hold" being my default recommendation in many instances. In Revolution's case, however, I consider its market cap to be unjustifiable. It appears to be based primarily on results from an early-stage trial.

I believe Revolution has more to prove before it merits such a lofty market cap; Accordingly, I rate it as a "Sell". In doing so, I am mindful of the uncertainties that cling to clinical stage biotechs, exacerbated here by the uncertain market environment.

data.title: Is The EU Financial Market On The Brink Of Change?
data.description:
By Sandrine Soubeyran, Director, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

The developments over recent months present a compelling narrative of potential transformation in the European financial landscape. While challenges persist, recent policy shifts, investments, and market recoveries signal a newfound resilience and optimism in the EU's economic prospects.

European economies have experienced weak economic growth since the Global Financial Crisis (GFC), but could this trend be about to change?

The EU's recovery has been sluggish since GFC and the Sovereign Debt Crisis. These crises severely impacted Greece, Spain, Ireland, Portugal, and Cyprus, forcing them to seek external financial assistance to refinance their government debt or rescue their banks. Greece, Portugal, and Ireland saw their sovereign debt downgraded to junk status as soaring interest rates made it increasingly challenging to manage budget deficits. In addition, Iceland, outside of the EU, had to intervene to bail out its banks. The EU economy only regained a state resembling pre-GFC conditions in 2017, but new challenges − Brexit in 2016, the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020, the war in Ukraine, and the Russian energy crisis in 2022 − continued to test the EU's resilience. These events left EU economic growth significantly weaker compared to the US and other global regions (Chart 1).

Chart 1: EU growth weakened by major political and financial events since GFC

Despite economic woes, European equities outperformed US and World ex US equities post GFC…

However, this pattern did not hold true for the European equity market. Despite enduring substantial economic challenges after GFC, the European equity market (as represented by the FTSE Europe Index) outperformed both the US and the World ex-US equity markets for about 10 years prior to 2015, as illustrated by Chart 2. The decisive actions of EU national governments, the IMF, and the EU played a pivotal role in bolstering financial markets and restoring investor confidence. These actions included creating new institutions to enhance financial sector stability, improving economic governance, establishing a safety net for sovereigns facing difficulties, and implementing structural reforms, particularly in the countries most affected.

…But not after Covid-19

However, unlike the market dynamics following GFC, the European equity market continued to underperform US equities during the post-Covid recovery. This disparity was not necessarily due to insufficient support from central banks or government interventions. Instead, the pandemic-induced reliance on technology during lockdowns and social distancing fuelled a rapid transition to a digital-first world. As a result, the expansion of artificial intelligence in offering data-driven solutions to address major challenges in managing the pandemic and other services from US Big Tech companies, and strong demand for their services, propelled the US technology sector to a significant rally, driving the overall outperformance of US equities.

Chart 2: European equity market rally in 2004-2015 after GFC replaced by US Tech boom post Covid-19

A new period for optimism?

Since February 2025, European equities seem to be gaining traction once again, with recent improvements in market performance potentially indicating a shift in leadership. Factors such as the new US administration's emphasis on reciprocal tariffs and developments from EU election outcomes have contributed to the EU's plans to embark on substantial infrastructure and defence spending. These developments have sparked renewed market optimism across Europe, especially in Germany. In France, financial markets also responded positively after the coalition government settled budgetary concerns, and President Macron announced €109 billion in artificial intelligence investments. In addition, the EU enabled member states to raise up to €650 billion by reactivating the national escape clause of the Stability and Growth Pact to boost defence spending. Combined with the extensive funding already available through the NextGeneration EU initiative to support the EU's green and digital transition since 2021, these cumulative investments have the potential to reshape the economic landscape and future of the European Union.

European equities have since outperformed the FTSE All World ex-US and Russell 1000. The strongest sectors include Alternative Energy, Aerospace & Defence, and Precious Metals and Mining − industries earmarked to benefit from new spending (Chart 3).

Chart 3: European equities boosted from surge in EU expenditure

European corporate bonds lifted by improved sentiment

Similar to European equities, Euro investment grade corporate bonds have also rallied, with spreads narrowing significantly compared to 7-10 year German government bonds, and certain sectors even trading through Bunds. Among these, Industrial Production corporate spreads have narrowed the most, followed by Energy and Manufacturing. Tighter corporate spreads can be attributed to a combination of lower interest rates, robust demand, and rising Bund yields. Bund yields rose after the German parliament decided to amend the debt brake, prompting concerns over increased issuance. These developments have driven euro corporate spreads to lows (Chart 4).

Chart 4: Industrial, energy and manufacturing euro corporate bonds buoyed by improving investor sentiment

Conclusion

The developments over recent months present a compelling narrative of potential transformation in the European financial landscape. While challenges persist, recent policy shifts, investments, and market recoveries signal a newfound resilience and optimism in the EU's economic prospects. With significant infrastructure and defence expenditures, as well as initiatives like the NextGeneration EU, the region appears poised to strengthen its position in global markets.

However, as with any economic transition, uncertainty lingers, and the true impact of these measures will take time to unfold. The extra 20% tariffs in the EU just implemented by the US at the time of writing, if left unchanged and lasting, will have a profound impact on trade and global growth. Investors must weigh the signs of opportunity against the backdrop of persistent global challenges. Could this indeed mark the beginning of a brighter era for the EU financial market? Only time will tell, but the groundwork for change is unmistakably being laid.

Disclaimer

LSEG includes (1) FTSE International Limited (“FTSE”), (2) Frank Russell Company (“Russell”), (3) FTSE Global Debt Capital Markets Inc. and FTSE Global Debt Capital Markets Limited (together, “FTSE Canada”), (4) FTSE Fixed Income Europe Limited (“FTSE FI Europe”), (5) FTSE Fixed Income LLC (“FTSE FI”), (6) FTSE (Beijing) Consulting Limited (“WOFE”) (7) Refinitiv Benchmark Services (UK) Limited (“RBSL”), (8) Refinitiv Limited (“RL”) and (9) Beyond Ratings S.A.S. data.title: ABB Ltd: It's The Macroeconomy
data.description:
Depositary Receipts are great, hedged ones odd

There's a new form of depositary receipt out there. Invented just a few years back and, according to those who promote them, gaining in popularity all the time. This new one is the Canadian Depositary Receipt, the CDR. This solves a problem in that Canadian investors can now buy into global companies without worrying about C$ exchange rate problems.

This is clearly aimed at the retail investor - anyone in any size will already be happy to work in USD and institutions in € and £, CHF and so on. So, this instrument is clearly for that retail investor. Which has its merits. As I said when talking about the BMW CDR:

It's wholly and absolutely true that everyone invests too much at home and not enough internationally. This is what Adam Smith meant when he used "invisible hand" in Wealth of Nations. Even though foreign profits are higher than domestic, there will be some who "as if led by an invisible hand" will still prefer to invest at home. The modern implication of that is that we all do invest too much at home and not internationally. We should diversify our portfolios more across geography, politics, regulation, currencies and the rest.

CDRs allow, enable, more global diversification for Canadian investors without the hassles of requiring an international broker, swapping C$ into US$ and all the rest. But as I say, larger investors will already be happy dealing with those bureaucratic barriers, so the instrument just isn't aimed at them. That greater diversification for the Canadian retail investor is, at the system level, a good thing.

I've also wondered a bit about the design of the instrument itself. As I've said here about the Toyota CDR:

No, no, I grasp what they're doing, it's that what they're doing seems odd to me. For the CDR does not have a relationship to a fixed number of the underlying shares. Rather, as the C$ moves around in value so the number of shares the CDR represents changes. This strikes me as most odd. For, I would assume - and my assumptions can be wrong of course - that part of the point of diversification into foreign companies is to diversify across FX rates. So, I think that odd.

This combination, ease of global diversification while deliberately removing the FX diversification, just strikes me as odd. But perhaps the folk who designed these things grasp what Canadian investors want better than I do. Or even, they've grasped that better and ended up designing what is wanted rather than what makes sense - a C$ instrument, sure, but one that offers both FX and geographic diversification.

ABB Ltd (NEOE:ABB:CA)

Which brings us to one of the more recent of these CAD hedged CDRs, ABB Ltd:

As we can see, that's a fairly new market entrant. As we can also see, the volume is pretty much nothing. 200 pieces a day is why that price is pretty chunky in its movements.

As compared to the Swiss Exchange, the primary quotation:

Or London, which is trading the Swiss instrument in CHF:

Or, there's the New York ADR:

So, near half a million pieces a day in NY, London about a quarter million, we don't have to go into Swiss Francs to trade the ultimate underlying in order to be able to gain volume. Which then also means closer spreads, ease of buying or selling in size and so on.

So what?

The importance of all of the above is that this new instrument, ABB:CA, is designed for one small subset of investors. It's for Canadian retail investors and, really, no one else. I still think it's odd that about the CAD hedging, but there we all are.

Anyone not a Canadian retail investor can and should simply ignore this new instrument. No, there will not even be arbitrage opportunities. Because the low volume will mean spreads are wide enough to choke those off. There's not even using it as a C$ FX proxy, as that's precisely what they prevent by having the CAD hedge.

So, ABB itself

From the accounts:

They've clearly vast, obviously profitable, and given what they consider to be a key figure, more than somewhat infested with DEI and the rest. You can guess my view on all that from that phrasing - I like companies to make money, and I'll deal with my ethics myself, thank you - but that's not anything other than a piece of passing snark.

The ABB dichotomy

As an investment proposition, ABB has two sides to it. One is that it's a great big boring global company. They're excellent at the sort of large engineering projects that they do. So, as a part of a 20-year portfolio - the one that's locked away in the back drawer - they're fine. We should have holdings in those sorts of companies. They're a bellwether for the global economy.

The other is that it's a great, big, boring global company. And here this specific instrument is destined to be low liquidity, limited as it is to Canadian retail investors. So there's no point in trying to trade in and out of it - which might even be a temptation with the more liquid Swiss, American or London quotes - and thus not really something for us here at Seeking Alpha where - alpha being the name for this idea - we are trying to beat that market, not match it. If we really just wanted a balanced global portfolio, then it would be Vanguard no load funds.

ABB itself

ABB is a large company operating in the global economy. OK, that's obvious enough. But this is a useful little proof:

That 25% (and it is, from $60 -ish to $45 -ish on this, the US quote) is nothing to do with ABB directly. This is global markets having conniption fits about tariffs. How this will interrupt global trade and so on. Rather more than many manufacturers, ABB will be less disrupted - they manufacture in many places after all. But, this is the general market view of what those macroeconomic events are going to do. So, down goes the ABB price.

The lesson for us here is that ABB is, largely, a stock driven by the macroeconomic forces. It's in many different businesses in many different places. We can talk about how data centres impact them, for example:

Data center spending remains robust, and that complements ABB’s strength in a range of medium-voltage product categories. While Eaton and Schneider are better-leveraged to data centers right now, the latter particularly on the back of strength in uninterruptible power supplies, rack power distribution units, and management software, ABB has been investing in expanding its offerings (particularly in UPS). At the same time, data center power demands are becoming more intense, driving more opportunities for medium-voltage equipment (relative to low-voltage).

Well, yes, OK, and we've also just recently had Microsoft backpedalling on up to $80 billion of data center commitments:

Microsoft Corp. has pulled back on data center projects around the world, suggesting the company is taking a harder look at its plans to build the server farms powering artificial intelligence and the cloud. The software company has recently halted talks for, or delayed development of, sites in Indonesia, the UK, Australia, Illinois, North Dakota and Wisconsin, according to people familiar with the situation.

That Chinese AI model that was hugely cheaper to train and then vastly cheaper to run showed that perhaps brute computing force wasn't wholly the answer to AI. And, if ABB has a business aiding in equipping data centers, then this isn't good news for ABB.

We can run through the business sector by sector (as here, a good analytical piece) and tot up how we think each individual piece is going to go. But we very rapidly get into confusion given the geographic spread and also the business verticals. It's not even obvious that the same events would strike all sectors the same way.

There's a point in such complexity that we should be thinking about other peoples' views. As in:

Remember, we should always downgrade analyst recommendations by a notch. So we've a clear majority - when adjusted - for not being in ABB.

Or, we can look at the yield. A mature and large company, should be paying a decent income, right? 2.11% doesn't quite meet that objective.

We're not going to hold ABB for the yield, that is. There's no one specific business line within the overall that drives the growth of the company. Each sectoral line has its pluses and minuses. The correct estimation of ABB's prospects is that it will grow, as with the economy globally, plus or minus a bit either way. Thus, it's the macroeconomic factors that particularly matter to that overall share price. As shown by the meltdown this past couple of weeks.

Therefore, the company advice is as above, perhaps a core holding for a long term, decades-long, position. Or, given that it will be inside any index fund positions, hold it that way. This specific instrument being analysed here is for Canadian retail investors only.

Why am I wrong?

Sure, I could be wrong about ABB. We are electrifying the world and this is an area ABB is in. And it isn't going to be some cute start up that - say and just as an example - lays 4,000 km of HVDC cable to bring solar power from Africa to Europe. It doesn't have to be ABB, either, but it'll be someone like ABB.

That repository of large-scale engineering expertise is valuable and will make returns. Greater than market? Could be.

My opinion

This piece is not about ABB so much as about the particular instrument, the ABB CAD hedged CDR. I think it's odd that it has the CAD hedge even while I grasp the value of there being a CDR in C$. But the combination - the design as a whole - is purely for Canadian retail investors. There is no point in anyone else even thinking about it.

So, for everyone else who is not a Canadian retail investor, this instrument is not for them. If you are Canadian, investing and retail then, perhaps. But as I say, even then, this is a back of the drawer, even boring, part of the long-term portfolio, not something to trade. And, if we're honest, there are better back of the drawer, long term, investments out there. Like those no load funds.

data.title: Tariffs Add New Hurdle To Private Equity's Exit Challenge
data.description:
Aggressive US tariffs are adding complexity to private equity's most pressing challenge: exits.

The industry entered 2025 with high hopes for improving exit conditions, encouraged by a 5% year-over-year uptick in the number of buyout fund exits in 2024. But uncertainty slowed the pace of exits in the first quarter, and fund managers are evaluating the effect of President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs announced April 2.

Private equity firms positioning portfolio companies for sale need to quickly gain an in-depth understanding of those assets' exposure to tariffs, said Kevin Desai, who advises private equity firms as PwC's sector leader for the industry. Particularly for portfolio companies with complicated supply chains, uncertainty concerning tariff impacts threatens to extend sale processes and reduce the sellers' return on investment.

"You have to put a box around the risk," Desai said.

Prepping portfolios

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) acknowledged in a 10-K filing earlier this year that tariffs "have the potential to increase costs, decrease margins, reduce the competitiveness of products and services offered by portfolio companies and adversely affect the revenues and profitability of portfolio companies."

The firm did not respond to a request for comment.

Many private equity managers understand their direct exposure to tariffs via portfolio companies and their suppliers, said John Fiorentino, a managing director in Alvarez & Marsal's private equity improvement practice. However, fewer managers have dug down to the level of their suppliers' suppliers, Fiorentino said.

"What you don't know is the inflationary exposure that's underneath that, when your supplier now passes on a 25% [price] increase," Fiorentino said.

Companies are poring over supplier contracts and optimizing existing supply chains. But many are delaying significant investments until they know whether steep tariffs are here to stay, or just the brash opening to further trade negotiations with US trading partners.

"The uncertainty is huge. I can't think of a single example in 2025 where somebody has made truly transformative changes to their supply chain," Fiorentino said.

Deals and volatility

Optimism for private equity deals in 2025 was widespread. More than 70% of the private equity and venture capital general partners surveyed by S&P Global Market Intelligence in late 2024 and early 2025 predicted private equity deal activity would improve this year.

New investment activity has been robust in the first quarter, with private equity deals totaling $221 billion globally, up 67% over the first quarter of 2024, according to Market Intelligence data.

Deal momentum could continue. Private equity typically turns volatility into its advantage, scooping up companies with valuations dented by market fluctuations. As KKR CFO Robert Lewin told a conference audience in February, the long-dated nature of private equity investments means managers can buy low and ride out economic cycles — or presidential administrations — until the opportune exit moment.

"In a lot of ways, volatility, while it could be painful to go through, could certainly be beneficial to our business and has been, if you look at past moments of high volatility over time," Lewin said.

Focus on fundamentals

The prospect of a tariff war comes as the industry faces increasing pressure to produce exits and return capital to investors.

Exit activity throttled by higher interest rates and a stubborn buyer-seller divide was showing signs of improvement in late 2024, when the average holding period for a buyout fund portfolio company was 6.1 years compared with 6.2 years in 2023, a 10-year high, according to Preqin Pro data.

Managing portfolios during a slow exit environment likely prepared private equity fund managers for this moment, according to Patrick Quay, Americas strategy and transactions private equity leader for EY-Parthenon.

"In concert with hold times extending, you've seen a much larger movement towards true underlying value creation by private equity managers, and not just financial engineering," Quay said.

That focus on fundamentals should have private equity set up to execute on exits, with secondary sales and trade sales to strategic acquirers likely to recover first, Quay said.

Private equity-backed IPOs — which numbered 212 globally in 2024, the most since 2021 — may take until late 2025 or 2026 to recover while sponsors ride out the current market volatility.

"The underlying conditions for private equity to both deploy new capital and to exit existing positions are still there. The tariffs will put a pause on that, but I still expect 2025 activity to be in excess of 2024 by the end of the year," Quay said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Returns for less than one year are not annualized. ITD is inception to date.

Class I shares may not be available to all investors. Minimum investments for the I share class may be higher than those for other classes.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than quoted. For performance current to the most recent month end, visit Leading Global Asset Management Firm for Investors Worldwide or call 877-215-1330. The maximum sales charge for the Fund's A shares is 4.50%. There is no up-front sales charge for class I shares. Thornburg Investment Management and/or Thornburg Securities LLC have contractually agreed to waive fees and reimburse expenses through at least 1 February 2025, for some of the share classes; these are reflected in the net expense ratio. For more detailed information on fund expenses and waivers/reimbursements, please see the fund's prospectus.

Market Review

The re-election of former president Donald Trump boosted U.S. equities amid expectations of a business-friendly administration taking over in Washington. A new de-regulation drive and potential tax cuts may help sustain U.S. economic resilience but tariffs and a gaping fiscal deficit make fixed income markets nervous.

China’s recovery in domestic consumption proved elusive as weakness in its property sector lingered. While Beijing’s fiscal and monetary stimulus helped, structural reforms remain pending. Tensions with the incoming Trump administration also weighed on sentiment. In Europe, elevated energy costs continued to hit industry and contributed to somewhat sticky inflation. Nonetheless, the European Central Bank (ECB) lowered its key rate four times in the second half of 2024, even as it raised or held steady its core inflation projections.

The Bank of Japan (BOJ), meanwhile, ended its negative rate policy last spring, hiking its key rate for the first time in 17 years. The BOJ lifted it again in July but held it steady at 0.25% in December, given the yen volatility and economic uncertainties more generally.

The AI theme continued to buoy the tech sector not just in the U.S., but globally as well. Demand for AI infrastructure-related hardware and software continued to run strong as the hyperscalers devote massive capex for the buildout, which promises real productivity enhancements down the road.

The fourth quarter of 2024 proved challenging for fixed income investors, as a surprising surge in government bond yields overshadowed the Federal Reserve's rate cuts. Despite the easing of monetary policy, the resilience of the U.S. economy and persistent inflation fueled expectations of a less accommodative stance in the future, driving yields higher and leading to negative returns across fixed income assets. The 10-year Treasury yield climbed 79 basis points during the quarter which reflected the market's anticipation of sustained economic strength and potential inflationary pressures. While shorter-term bonds saw some benefit from the Fed's actions, the overall sentiment in the fixed income market remained cautious in light of the evolving economic landscape.

Fourth Quarter 2024 Performance Highlights

In 4Q24 the portfolio (I share class) returned -1.82%, 82 basis points ahead of the Blended Index (60% MSCI ACWI Index and 40% Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index). Year to date the portfolio (I share class) returned 11.41%, 192 basis points ahead of the Blended Index. Stock selection within financials and an underweight to consumer staples contributed positively to relative outperformance versus the MSCI ACWI Index. Stock selection within consumer discretionary and information technology detracted from relative performance. From a geographic perspective, stock selection within the United States and India contributed to the portfolio’s relative performance. Stock selection from Denmark and Brazil detracted from relative performance.

Current Positioning and Outlook

Global equity markets experienced a volatile 2024, climbing through the first three quarters of the year before selling off during the fourth quarter. Our portfolio positioning entering the quarter, which included a relative underweight to equities, continued to reflect caution about historically high valuations for U.S. equities despite an improving but still uncertain economic picture. While our underweight to equities bolstered relative results during the periods, our bias towards non-U.S. equities was a headwind. Large valuation gaps between the U.S. and the rest of the world present potential for relative value and diversification benefits, and while we are not making a macro call on specific regions, we believe the international landscape offers compelling opportunities for stock pickers.

Within fixed income, higher bond yields are presenting a better setup for fixed income, but credit spreads remain tighter than their historical averages. While we continue to take a defensive posture both from a rates and credit perspective, we are taking advantage of the higher income opportunities that bonds are now offering the portfolio.

While markets may remain volatile, we believe our disciplined, flexible, process of identifying attractive relative value opportunities across equity and fixed income markets can help us effectively navigate the current environment.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Important Information

Unless otherwise noted, the source of all data, charts, tables and graphs is Thornburg Investment Management, Inc., as of 31 December 2024.

Investments carry risks, including possible loss of principal. Additional risks may be associated with investments outside the United States, especially in emerging markets, including currency fluctuations, illiquidity, volatility, and political and economic risks. Investments in small- and mid-capitalization companies may increase the risk of greater price fluctuations. Portfolios investing in bonds have the same interest rate, inflation, and credit risks that are associated with the underlying bonds. The value of bonds will fluctuate relative to changes in interest rates, decreasing when interest rates rise. Investments in the Fund are not FDIC insured, nor are they bank deposits or guaranteed by a bank or any other entity.

The views expressed are subject to change and do not necessarily reflect the views of Thornburg Investment Management, Inc. This information should not be relied upon as a recommendation or investment advice and is not intended to predict the performance of any investment or market.

Funds invested in a limited number of holdings may expose an investor to greater volatility. There is no guarantee that the Fund will meet its investment objectives.

Any securities, sectors, or countries mentioned are for illustration purposes only. Holdings are subject to change. Under no circumstances does the information contained within represent a recommendation to buy or sell any security.

Basis Point (bp) – A unit equal to 1/100th of 1%. 1% = 100 basis points (bps).

U.S. Treasury securities, such as bills, notes and bonds, are negotiable debt obligations of the U.S. government. These debt obligations are backed by the "full faith and credit" of the government and issued at various schedules and maturities. Income from Treasury securities is exempt from state and local, but not federal, taxes.

Thornburg Summit Fund's Blended Index is composed of 60% MSCI ACWI Index (MSCI ACWI NTR) and 40% Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (BBG Global-Agg TR Value), rebalanced monthly. The MSCI ACWI Index (MSCI ACWI NTR) is a market capitalization weighted index that is representative of the market structure of 47 developed and emerging market countries in North and South America, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and the Pacific Rim. The index is calculated with net dividends reinvested in U.S. dollars. The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (BBG Global-Agg TR Value) provides a broad-based measure of the global investment-grade fixed-rate debt markets.

The performance of any index is not indicative of the performance of any particular investment. Unless otherwise noted, index returns reflect the reinvestment of income dividends and capital gains, if any, but do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions or other expenses of investing. Investors may not make direct investments into any index.

Not FDIC Insured. May lose value. No bank guarantee.

Before investing, carefully consider the Fund’s investment goals, risks, charges and expenses. For a prospectus or summary prospectus containing this and other information, contact your financial advisor or visit thornburg.com. Read them carefully before investing.

Thornburg is a global investment firm delivering on strategy for institutions, financial professionals and investors worldwide. The privately held firm, founded in 1982, is an active, high-conviction manager of fixed income, equities, multi-asset solutions and sustainable investments with $45.3* billion in total assets across mutual funds, institutional accounts, separate accounts and UCITS.

*Includes assets under management ($44.1B) and assets under advisement ($1.2B).
